title: IMD says southwest monsoon likely to advance over Delhi, Haryana, Punjab in next two days - News On AIR
article_text:
The India Meteorological Department has said that the southwest monsoon is likely to advance over Delhi, Haryana, Chandigarh and Punjab during the next two days. Yesterday, it further advanced into some more parts of Madhya Pradesh, remaining parts of Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand and Bihar, some parts of Uttar Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh and Ladakh and most parts of Uttarakhand.

The weather agency has predicted heavy to very heavy rainfall at isolated places over Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Coastal Karnataka, East Madhya Pradesh, Kerala, Mahe, Konkan, Goa, Odisha, South Interior Karnataka, Uttarakhand, Vidarbha and West Uttar Pradesh today. The met department estimated heavy rainfall over Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Meghalaya, East Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, Haryana, Chandigarh and Delhi today. According to the IMD, similar weather conditions are predicted over Himachal Pradesh, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, North Interior Karnataka, Telangana, West Bengal, Sikkim and West Madhya Pradesh during the day.

The IMD has also predicted that during July, below-normal rainfall is likely over most parts of the country. However, some areas of Northwest and Northeast India, East-Central India and the eastern peninsular region are likely to receive normal to above-normal rainfall.

title: IndiaвҖҷs Monsoon Down 40% in June, July Offers No Relief, IMD Warns - Ground Report
article_text:
IndiaвҖҷs monsoon opened 2026 badly. June rainfall came in 40 percent below normal, the steepest early-season shortfall in years. Now the India Meteorological Department (IMD) says July offers little relief: rainfall will likely stay below normal across most of the country, while temperatures climb above normal almost everywhere.
Monsoon winds pushed into three of IndiaвҖҷs four rainfall regions by June 30, reaching more of Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh and Ladakh. But wind coverage didnвҖҷt bring rain.
Central India suffered the worst, down 50 percent for the month. East and northeast India followed at 40 percent below normal. The southern peninsula ran 27 percent short. Northwest India, where the monsoon hasnвҖҷt fully arrived, logged a 31 percent deficit вҖ” and most of what fell there came from pre-monsoon storms, not the monsoon itself.
By June 29, 76 percent of IndiaвҖҷs districts had recorded deficient rainfall or worse. Meghalaya fared worst among monsoon-covered states, down 76 percent. Manipur, Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh each ran roughly two-thirds short.
A strengthening El NiГұo is the main driver. The US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration declared El NiГұoвҖҷs onset on June 11 and expects it to strengthen into the winter of 2026-27. El NiГұo years typically suppress IndiaвҖҷs monsoon, and IMD had already built that into its forecast.
But El NiГұo isnвҖҷt working alone, said Raghu Murtugudde, a visiting professor at the Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur and emeritus professor at the University of Maryland. He told Down To Earth that warming patterns are reshaping wind shifts, while a pull from the Meiyu-Baiu front вҖ” a rainfall belt running from coastal China to Japan вҖ” is delaying the monsoonвҖҷs progress. вҖңThatвҖҷs why we have excess rains over the northwest and deficit over the core,вҖқ Murtugudde said.
He also pointed to a missing internal driver: weak circulation has starved the monsoonвҖҷs normal wet-dry cycle, the process that seeds new low-pressure systems every one to two weeks. Few systems have formed, so little rain has moved inland.
The seasonal picture isnвҖҷt dire yet. June normally supplies a fifth of the seasonвҖҷs rain, and the countryвҖҷs seasonal deficit sits at 8 percent, Murtugudde said. He noted that years shifting from a La NiГұa winter into an El NiГұo summer, like this one, tend to produce the steepest early deficits вҖ” sometimes up to 15 percent.
IMDвҖҷs July outlook, issued June 30, calls for monthly rainfall below 94 percent of the long-term average of 280.4 millimeters. Most of the country will stay dry. Exceptions include parts of Northwest and Northeast India, East-Central India and the eastern peninsula, where rain should run normal to above normal.
Heat will add to the strain. Daytime highs are expected above normal almost everywhere except a few pockets of west-central India. Nighttime lows will stay warm too, with only isolated relief in central and northeast India. IMD tied both the rain shortfall and the heat to the same strengthening El NiГұo, alongside neutral Indian Ocean Dipole conditions that offer no offsetting boost.
There are signs of a turn. Murtugudde said the monsoon trough could get a lift from low-pressure systems forming in the Bay of Bengal and over land this week. вҖңNow that the core zone is firing up, we should get a good pull on the trough to move forward,вҖқ he said.
Independent forecaster Navdeep Dahiya, writing on X on June 30, called it вҖңa super crucial week for India as monsoon dynamics are finally showing up on wind patterns.вҖқ He pointed to a dual low-pressure setup вҖ” one inland, one over the Bay of Bengal вҖ” plus an established monsoon axis across north India.
IMDвҖҷs own release backed that read, noting a cyclonic circulation over the north Bay of Bengal expected to develop into a low-pressure area near the northwest Bay of Bengal around July 3.
Farmers, water managers and power planners are watching that system closely. A weak monsoon strains irrigation timing, reservoir levels and hydropower output alike. IMD urges water conservation and contingency planning now, rather than waiting to see if JulyвҖҷs rain arrives. The agency will issue its outlook for August and September in late July вҖ” the next real test of whether this monsoon catches up or falls further behind.

title: Planning a monsoon trip? Don't miss these breathtaking seasonal waterfalls - MSN
article_text:
India is blessed with breathtaking natural beauty, and among its most magical creations are its waterfalls. From thunderous cascades hidden in dense forests to delicate streams flowing through misty valleys, every waterfall has a charm of its own. If you are someone who loves nature, adventure, and peaceful escapes, visiting these waterfalls at least once in your lifetime should definitely be on your travel bucket list. Here are the top five waterfalls in India that promise unforgettable moments and stunning views.
1. Dudhsagar Waterfall, GoaГўВҖВ“Karnataka Border
Dudhsagar, meaning ГўВҖВңSea of Milk,ГўВҖВқ is one of IndiaГўВҖВҷs most iconic waterfalls. As the water gushes down from a height of over 300 meters, it creates a white, milky appearance that looks nothing less than magical. What makes Dudhsagar even more special is its setting. Surrounded by thick forests and located right next to a railway bridge, it feels like a scene straight out of an adventure movie.
Whether you trek through the lush Bhagwan Mahavir Wildlife Sanctuary or take a jeep trail, the journey itself is as breathtaking as the waterfall.
Jog Falls is one of the tallest waterfalls in India, and it is famous for the sheer force with which the Sharavathi River plunges down the rocky cliffs. The four sections of the waterfallГўВҖВ”Raja, Rani, Rocket, and RoarerГўВҖВ”make the sight even more dramatic.

other_info.csv_title: Planning a monsoon trip? Don't miss these breathtaking seasonal waterfalls - MSN

title: Odisha Monsoon Forecast: IMD Predicts Three Days of Heavy Rain Across State - pragativadi.com
article_text:
Odisha is bracing for continuous rainfall over the next three days, bringing relief to farmers worried by JuneвҖҷs 47 percent rainfall deficit.

The India Meteorological Department (IMD) predicted widespread heavy showers starting July 3 as a lowвҖ‘pressure area forms over the Bay of Bengal.

IMD DirectorвҖ‘General Mrutyunjay Mohapatra said the system will trigger moderate to very heavy rain across most districts until July 5. Coastal and interior Odisha are expected to receive aboveвҖ‘normal rainfall, with some areas likely to experience intense downpours. Rain has already continued since June 30 in Bhubaneswar and Cuttack.

The lowвҖ‘pressure system developing over the northвҖ‘west Bay of Bengal is expected to move westвҖ‘northwestward, enhancing monsoon activity. Mohapatra noted that the stateвҖҷs highlands and Eastern Ghats will see stronger rainfall due to their interaction with monsoon winds.

The IMD clarified that the system is unlikely to deepen into a depression, but its gradual intensification will sustain heavy rain. The first two weeks of July are crucial for paddy cultivation, helping farmers begin transplanting and sowing after a dry June.

Global weather patterns also indicate a weak El NiГұo phase strengthening through July and August, while the Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) is turning positive, which could offset El NiГұoвҖҷs impact and support good rainfall in September and October.

title: IMD Forecasts Heavy to Very Heavy Rainfall in Northeast; Heatwave Conditions Likely in Parts of West India - News On AIR
article_text:
India Meteorological Department has forecast fairly widespread to widespread rainfall along with thunderstorm, lightning and gusty winds with heavy to very heavy rainfall likely over Sub Himalayan West Bengal and Sikkim and northeast India during the week. Isolated extremely heavy rainfall was witnessed over Assam and Meghalaya and Sub Himalayan West Bengal and Sikkim today.

Maximum temperatures were in the range of 40 to 46 degree Celsius over most parts of the country except western Himalayan region, northeast Bihar, and northeast India, where they were below 36 degrees. The highest maximum temperature of 45.6 degrees was reported at Banda in Uttar Pradesh.

Isolated to scattered light to moderate rainfall with thunderstorm, lightning and gusty winds are likely over Punjab, Haryana, Chandigarh and Delhi tomorrow and during 2nd to 5th May. Meanwhile, heat wave conditions very likely in some places of Vidarbha today and in West Rajasthan during 1st to 3rd May. Hot and humid weather conditions are very likely to prevail in isolated pockets over Odisha from today till 1st May.
